medications.drug_name: IM hyoscine / SL ondansetron over chat
medications.indication_rationale: Injectable/parenteral symptom control cannot be given via telehealth and would not address dehydration or exclude a surgical abdomen; the patient needs assessment.
Begin slow sips of oral rehydration solution as an interim measure while arranging urgent assessment; likely needs IV fluids given near-syncope.
Given a food-triggered reaction with flushing/GI/near-syncope and MCAS, ensure an adrenaline autoinjector and action plan; use adrenaline if airway/circulatory anaphylaxis features develop.
medications.dose_route_frequency: as per action plan if anaphylaxis develops

medications.indication_rationale: Prolonged fasting is not appropriate in a dehydrated patient needing assessment; PEA/zinc have no acute evidence base.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: GI dietitian for food-reactivity management once stabilised.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Slow oral rehydration and trigger-food avoidance
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Rehydration and avoiding identified triggers are sensible; rehydration is interim to assessment.
No evidence for acute management; not recommended.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch for throat/breathing symptoms, true collapse, a rigid abdomen, blood in stool, or stopping passing urine, and call 000 (and use adrenaline) if they occur; keep sipping rehydration fluids.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Safety-nets anaphylaxis, surgical abdomen and severe dehydration.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Sit/lie to avoid fainting, and arrange for someone to be with you or take you for assessment.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces fall/collapse risk in an isolated patient.

patient_education_points.point: Your gut's mast cells have over-reacted to a food, causing violent cramps, bloating and diarrhoea, and you're losing a lot of fluid — that's why you feel faint and your heart is racing.

Because you're near-fainting, losing fluid and on your own, you need to be assessed in person today — likely for a drip and to check your tummy properly. Sip rehydration fluids in the meantime, not just water.
Since this was food-triggered with flushing and faintness, make sure you have an adrenaline pen and plan — and call 000 (and use it) if your throat tightens, you wheeze, or you collapse.
Go to ED / call 000 if your stomach becomes hard like a board, you pass blood, or you can't pass urine.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent same-day in-person/ED assessment; GP/allergy review afterwards for H1/H2 blockers, cromolyn and dietitian
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Hydration/vitals, abdominal status, tryptase/anaphylaxis assessment, mast cell management plan
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Airway/breathing symptoms, collapse, rigid abdomen or GI bleeding — call 000.

must_include_items: Arrange urgent in-person/ED assessment for abdominal review and IV rehydration
Screen anaphylaxis (airway/circulation) and ensure an adrenaline autoinjector/action plan
Do NOT provide remote injections or advise prolonged fasting in a dehydrated patient
Safety-net surgical abdomen, GI bleeding, collapse and anuria
acceptable_alternatives: Interim oral rehydration while arranging assessment
Ambulance if the patient cannot safely attend or collapses
errors_of_commission: Advising the patient to ride it out at home
Prolonged fasting or unproven agents as therapy
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires (1) urgent in-person/ED assessment for the abdomen and IV rehydration, (2) an anaphylaxis screen with adrenaline plan, and (3) no remote injections. Managing near-syncope with heavy fluid loss remotely is a fail.
